title: Catechist Guide — Divine Revelation
html:
Catechist Guide

For preparing and leading the class. For catechist use only.

Class Summary

Estimated duration: 50-60 minutes (with activities, 90 minutes)

Central theme: God reveals himself to human beings throughout history, culminating in Jesus Christ, the definitive Word. This revelation is transmitted by Tradition, Scripture, and the Magisterium.

Objectives:

  • That the student understand that revelation is God's initiative, not human discovery.
  • That they know the main stages of God's revelation in the Old Testament.
  • That they discover Jesus Christ as the full and definitive revelation of God.
  • That they understand how revelation is transmitted to us: Tradition, Scripture, Magisterium.

Key verse: Hebrews 1:1-2 («Long ago at many times... he has spoken to us by a Son»)

Catechism references: CCC 50-73, 74-100

Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. Welcome and icebreaker 5 min Greet, create atmosphere. Question: «What does it mean to you when someone ‘reveals’ or ‘opens up’ to you?»
2. The burning bush 10 min Read Ex 3:1-6 aloud. Ask: «What is special about this story?» Point out the dynamic: God's initiative, personal call, mission.
3. Main content 20 min Present sections 1-4. Read CCC 50-51, 65-66. Emphasize that Christ is the definitive revelation. Speak about Tradition, Scripture, Magisterium.
4. Group activity 10 min Choose one workbook activity: reading of Ex 3 (if adults/youth) or timeline of revelation (if children).
5. Sharing 8 min Students share what they discovered (optional, don't force).
6. Closing prayer 4 min Pray the class prayer together. Remember Hebrews 1:1-2. Invite them to read Exodus 3 during the week.

Notes for the Catechist

About the history of revelation

This topic is fundamental for all subsequent catechesis. If the student does not understand that God takes the initiative and that Jesus Christ is the definitive revelation, the other classes will lose their foundation. Take the time necessary to make this clear.

The story of the burning bush is not a children's tale. It is the foundational moment of revelation. Moses is not a hero who discovers God through his intelligence: he is a failed shepherd whom God chooses. This is important: God does not choose the most capable, but those who are available. «Here I am» is the response God expects from each one.

About Saint Paul's conversion

The story of Paul on the road to Damascus is the paradigm of revelation in the New Testament. Just as Moses was knocked from his security (he was a prince in Egypt and lost everything), Paul is knocked from his horse. In both cases, God intervenes when man least expects it. You can use this parallelism in your presentation.

About Tradition, Scripture, and Magisterium

This is perhaps the most technical point of the class. Don't get tangled in details. The key image is that of the deposit: revelation is a treasure that the Church receives, guards, transmits, and interprets. The three channels are not three separate things; they are aspects of the same reality.

A useful analogy: imagine a family recipe passed down through generations. There is the oral tradition (how grandmother taught mother), the cookbook where it is written (Scripture), and grandmother herself who says «this is done this way and not another» (the Magisterium). All three are necessary for the recipe to reach the next generation correctly.

Adaptation by age

Seed (children, 7-12): Focus on the story of the burning bush. Use images of the Byzantine icon. Ask: «How do you think Moses felt?» The activity of drawing the bush is ideal. Do not go into details about Tradition and Magisterium. Do mention that the Bible is God's Word.

Shoots (youth, 13-17): The parallel between Moses' story and their own works well. Ask: «Have you felt God calling you in some way?» Paul's conversion can connect with their experiences of radical change. Introduce the topic of Tradition and Scripture with questions like: «How do we know what is true?»

Root (new adults): Skepticism about revelation is normal. Address the question: «In what sense is the Bible God's Word?» Explain inspiration. Use the recipe analogy for Tradition/Scripture/Magisterium. Speak of Vatican II and Dei Verbum if the group is receptive.

Tree (formed adults): Deepen the relationship between Scripture and Tradition, the historical debate with the Reformation, the Council of Trent and Vatican II. Also explore the implicit Christology: Jesus is not one prophet among many, he is the Word itself. Discuss the affirmation of CCC 66 (no other public revelation) in contrast with private revelations.

Supplementary resources

  • YouCat 5-12: Questions about revelation, Tradition, Scripture, and Magisterium. Excellent for adolescents.
  • Dei Verbum (Dogmatic Constitution on Divine Revelation): Document of Vatican II. Chapters I and II are the most relevant. Available at vatican.va.
  • Opus Dei, «The Revelation of God»: Theme from the series «Themes of Christian Faith» at opusdei.org.
  • Introduction to Christianity by Joseph Ratzinger: Chapter on revelation as «unveiling.»
  • Recommended film: Paul, Apostle of Christ (2018) to visualize Paul's conversion. Watch only selected scenes.

Difficult Questions (FAQ with answers)

«Why didn't God reveal himself all at once?»

Excellent question. God reveals himself gradually because he respects our capacity to understand. A father does not explain the theory of relativity to his three-year-old child; he speaks in a way the child can understand, and year after year he goes deeper. So it is with God and humanity: he was preparing us, educating our capacity to receive the full revelation. Furthermore, the gradualness shows that God wants a love story with humanity, not an «instruction manual» delivered all at once. God's patience is pedagogical.

«Is the Bible God's Word or the words of men?»

Both. The Church teaches that the Bible has God as its principal author, but was written by human authors who used their own language, style, and culture. This is possible because the Holy Spirit «inspired» the human authors, guiding them to write all and only what God wanted. It is like the Incarnation: Jesus Christ is true God and true man. The Bible, in an analogous way, is truly God's Word and truly the words of men. This means that to understand it well, we need to know both the historical context and the faith of the Church.

«How do we distinguish between public revelation and private revelations?»

Public revelation is what God made to humanity through the prophets and especially in Jesus Christ. It is contained in Scripture and Tradition and is for everyone. Private revelations (such as those at Fatima, Lourdes, Saint Margaret Mary) add nothing new to public revelation. Their function is to help us live it better in a specific time. That is why the Church can approve private revelations without obliging anyone to believe in them. The essentials are already said in Christ.

«Isn't it arrogant to say that only Christianity has the complete revelation?»

It is not a matter of arrogance, but of fidelity to what Christ himself affirmed. Jesus did not present himself as one teacher among many, but as the Son of God, the only way to the Father (Jn 14:6). If he is who he says he is, then he is the definitive revelation. This does not mean despising other religions: Vatican II recognizes that they «often reflect a ray of that Truth which enlightens all men» (Nostra Aetate 2). But for a Christian, the fullness of truth is in Christ.

«What happens to people who never heard of Christ?»

God does not let himself be outdone in generosity. The Church teaches that those who, through no fault of their own, do not know the Gospel or Christ —but seek God with sincere heart and strive to do his will as they understand it— can attain salvation (CCC 847, citing Lumen Gentium 16). This does not make evangelization superfluous. On the contrary: it is one more reason to share the good news.

Catechist's Prayer

Lord, you who revealed yourself to Moses in the burning bush
and called Saint Paul on the road to Damascus,
reveal yourself also to those who will hear me today.
May my words be transparent to your Word.
May I not obstruct, but point toward you.
Give me the humility of Moses, who said «Here I am,»
and the ardor of Paul, who left everything for you.
Amen.
